texts.full_text: and equipping a valve in one of the outlet pipes to open and close the one outlet pipe. 2. Description of Related Art RRecent advancements have introduced various innovations for reducing noise emissions from vehicle exhaust systems, especially focusing on low-frequency noises which are more challenging due to their penetrating nature that can disturb passengers as well as residents in surrounding areas when vehicles operate at certain engine speeds or conditions. In conventional dual-exhaust system designs without specific adaptations for CDA (Cylinder Deactivation) engines, the reduction of active cylinders during partial load operation leads to imbalanced exhaust pulses and resonance issues within one branch. This imbalance can cause significant low-frequency noise because deactivating a portion of an eight-cylinder engine effectively turns it into four or six cylinder mode in terms of its firing pattern. Therefore, there is a need for designing dual-exhaust systems specifically equipped with mechanisms that mitigate these effects during CDA operation to ensure smooth exhaust flow and reduced acoustic emissions. Solutions have been proposed such as variable geometry silencers which can alter their internal configuration based on the engine’s operating condition; however, they tend to be complex in structure or costly. However, none of the existing solutions provide a
